next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

Elsie woke before the light had chosen a shape. The room was all one color, a thin iron gray, and the blanket over her had gone damp where two breaths had soaked it through. Her left hip hurt from the bed frame. Her mouth tasted of ash and salt and the tannin of tea gone cold in a cup she had never finished.

Noreen lay on her side facing the window, knees pulled up, one hand under her cheek. In the half-dark she looked younger and more tired at the same time. Her hair had come loose from its pins in the night, black against the pillow, and there was a red mark at her shoulder where Elsie had held her too hard and not let go. Elsie knew the exact shape of that mark because she had put it there with her own hand, with her own hunger. She had not been drunk. She had not mistaken a body for a blanket. She had known what she was doing each second of it.

The floorboard outside the room gave one long complaint. Then came a cough from down the landing, a man's cough trying not to be loud. Arthur. Billeted men learned to cough politely, as if lungs could be taught manners.

Noreen opened her eyes.

"He's up," Elsie whispered.

"I heard." Noreen's voice was rough from sleep. She did not pull away. She looked straight at Elsie, then at Elsie's mouth, then back to her eyes. "You're shaking. That's cold, not regret, yeah?"

Elsie put her hand under the blanket and found Noreen's wrist. The skin was warm, pulse quick. "Both," she said, because anything else would have sounded like acting.

Noreen gave a breath that was almost a laugh, almost not. "Fair enough. Come on, th...
open_hooks_to_carry: Power fails and both households consolidate around one fire
Physical comfort shifts into deliberate intimacy with clear mutual consent
prev_tail_excerpt:
"This isn't just him breaking your heart a bit more. He's signing you into his debts. Don't let him talk you down from that."

Elsie held the back of her chair until her knuckles blanched. She wanted to say I know. She wanted to say I have always known. Both would have been lies.

"I won't," she said.

Noreen picked up the blue saucepan, now emptied of all purpose except carrying home a story she could not tell aloud, and moved to the door. Before stepping out she turned back.

"If it goes bad tonight, knock once on the wall, twice on the pipe. I'll hear."

"Even with Arthur there?"

"Especially with Arthur there. He hears what he hears and keeps his own counsel."

"That sounds like trust."

"It sounds like a man who labels his tea bags by day so he doesn't waste them." Noreen's mouth twitched. "I trust habits before I trust speeches."

Then she was gone into the lane, boots crunching on old ice.

Elsie stood in the center of her kitchen and listened until the sound vanished.

She looked at the cupboard door, swollen wood, flaking paint, rusted hinge that never sat true. She had cursed that door for two winters and meant to fix it each spring. It had stuck, then given, then emptied its mouth over her knees.

By the time Tom came home she had the tea laid, the slips hidden, the sums copied, and a new map in her head.

The map had no easy roads on it. It had no husband-shaped shelter marked in safe ink.

It had dates, names, and one clear fact: if she did nothing, the debt would not stay his.
state_deltas_required: Survive a full-night blackout without panic.
Elsie and Noreen become physically intimate, creating a new relational center that cannot be undone.
